long_reaction_chains.name: Long Reaction Chains
long_reaction_chains.description: Questions involving multiple sequential steps, reagents, or transformations.

structural_patterns: Product of one step becomes reactant of next

counting_enumeration.name: Counting/Enumeration
counting_enumeration.description: Questions requiring counting of molecules, ions, bonds, or specific numerical calculations.

deep_mechanistic_reasoning.name: Deep Mechanistic Reasoning
deep_mechanistic_reasoning.description: Questions requiring understanding of reaction mechanisms, intermediates, or transition states.

comparative_ranking.name: Comparative Ranking
comparative_ranking.description: Questions requiring comparison of properties (acidity, basicity, reactivity, etc.) and arranging in orders.

questions.question_text: Williamson synthesis of preparing unsymmetrical ether is: (A) SN1 reaction (B) SN2 reaction (C) Electrophilic addition reaction (D) Elimination reaction
questions.topic: Organic Chemistry - Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers
questions.correct_answer: B
questions.solution_summary: Williamson synthesis involves the reaction of an alkoxide ion with a primary alkyl halide via SN2 mechanism. The alkoxide attacks the alkyl halide from the back side, displacing the halide.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding of the mechanism of Williamson synthesis, which proceeds via SN2.

questions.question_text: Which of the following compounds would be hydrolysed by aqueous KOH most easily? (A) CH2=CH-Br (B) CH3-CH2-Br (C) CH3-CHBr-CH3 (D) CH2=CH-CH2-Br
questions.correct_answer: D
questions.solution_summary: Allylic halides (CH2=CH-CH2-Br) undergo SN1/SN2 reactions readily due to the stability of the allylic carbocation and the allylic position. Aryl and vinyl halides are unreactive; secondary halides are less reactive than allylic.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires comparing reactivity of different types of halides based on carbocation stability and bond strength.

questions.question_text: According to Werner's theory of coordination compounds: (A) Primary valences are ionisable. (B) Secondary valences are ionisable. (C) Both primary and secondary valences are nonionisable. (D) Both primary and secondary valences are ionisable.
questions.correct_answer: A
questions.solution_summary: According to Werner's theory, primary valences (oxidation state) are ionisable, while secondary valences (coordination number) are non-ionisable.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Pure recall of Werner's theory about primary and secondary valences.

questions.question_text: Which of the following complex ion is not optically active? (A) [Co(ox)3]^3- (B) cis-[Co(en)2Cl2]^+ (C) trans-[Co(en)2Cl2]^+ (D) [Co(en)3]^3+
questions.correct_answer: C
questions.solution_summary: trans-[Co(en)2Cl2]^+ has a plane of symmetry, making it optically inactive. cis-[Co(en)2Cl2]^+ and [Co(en)3]^3+ are optically active. [Co(ox)3]^3- is also optically active.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding of symmetry elements and optical activity in coordination compounds.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is the softest metal? (A) Zn (B) Sc (C) Cu (D) Fe
questions.correct_answer: A
questions.solution_summary: Zn is the softest among the given options due to its relatively weak metallic bonding (full d10 configuration, fewer unpaired electrons for metallic bonding).

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires comparing metallic properties of transition metals based on bonding.

questions.question_text: In the Hinsberg's method for separation of primary, secondary and tertiary amines, the reagent used is: (A) Nitrous acid (B) CHCl3 + aq. NaOH (C) Benzenesulphonyl chloride (D) Acetyl chloride
questions.correct_answer: C
questions.solution_summary: Hinsberg's reagent is benzenesulphonyl chloride (C6H5SO2Cl). It reacts differently with primary, secondary, and tertiary amines, allowing their separation.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Pure recall of the reagent used in the Hinsberg test.

questions.question_text: Which one of the following amines gives an alcohol on reaction with HNO2? [Image: (A) Aniline, (B) Methylamine, (C) N,N-dimethylaniline, (D) Tertiary amine]
questions.correct_answer: B
questions.solution_summary: Primary aliphatic amines react with HNO2 to give alcohols (with N2 evolution). Primary aromatic amines give diazonium salts. Secondary and tertiary amines give different products.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires knowledge of the reaction of different types of amines with nitrous acid.

questions.question_text: The freezing point of one molal KCl solution, assuming KCl to be completely dissociated in water, is: Kf for water = 1.86 K kg mol-1. (A) -3.72°C (B) +3.72°C (C) -1.86°C (D) +2.72°C
questions.correct_answer: A
questions.solution_summary: KCl dissociates into 2 ions (K+ and Cl-), so i = 2. ΔTf = i × Kf × m = 2 × 1.86 × 1 = 3.72°C. Freezing point = 0 - 3.72 = -3.72°C.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires application of freezing point depression formula with van't Hoff factor.

questions.question_text: A solution of acetone in ethanol: (A) obeys Raoult's law. (B) forms an ideal solution. (C) shows a positive deviation from Raoult's law. (D) shows a negative deviation from Raoult's law.
questions.correct_answer: C
questions.solution_summary: Acetone and ethanol form a non-ideal solution with positive deviation from Raoult's law. The interactions between acetone and ethanol are weaker than acetone-acetone and ethanol-ethanol interactions.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires knowledge of the type of deviation shown by acetone-ethanol mixture.

questions.question_text: Which of the following cell converts the energy of combustion of fuel into electrical energy? (A) Mercury cell (B) Fuel cell (C) Dry cell (D) Lead storage cell
questions.correct_answer: B
questions.solution_summary: A fuel cell directly converts the chemical energy of combustion of a fuel (like H2) and an oxidant (like O2) into electrical energy.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Pure recall of the definition of a fuel cell.

questions.question_text: The unit of rate and rate constant are same for a: (A) First order reaction (B) Second order reaction (C) Zero order reaction (D) Third order reaction
questions.sub_topic: Units of Rate and Rate Constant
questions.correct_answer: C
questions.solution_summary: For a zero order reaction, Rate = k. So the units of rate and rate constant are the same: mol L-1 s-1.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires knowledge of units of rate constants for different reaction orders.

questions.question_text: Pyranose ring of glucose is formed due to the reaction between: (A) C1 and C3 (B) C1 and C5 (C) C1 and C4 (D) C1 and C2
questions.correct_answer: B
questions.solution_summary: The pyranose ring of glucose is formed by the reaction between the aldehyde group at C1 and the -OH group at C5, forming a six-membered ring (pyranose).

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires recalling the formation of the pyranose ring in glucose.

questions.question_text: Assertion: Actinoids show wide range of oxidation states. Reason: Actinoids are radioactive in nature.
questions.correct_answer: B
questions.solution_summary: Assertion is true: actinoids show a wide range of oxidation states. Reason is also true: actinoids are radioactive, but this does not explain the wide range of oxidation states. The range is due to comparable energies of 5f, 6d, and 7s orbitals.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding the cause of wide oxidation states in actinoids.

questions.question_text: Assertion: Hydrolysis of an ester follows first order kinetics. Reason: The concentration of water does not get altered much during the reaction.
questions.correct_answer: A
questions.solution_summary: Assertion is true: hydrolysis of ester is a pseudo first order reaction. Reason is true and explains it: water is present in large excess, so its concentration remains practically constant, making the reaction kinetically first order.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding of pseudo first order reactions and the concept of excess reagent.

questions.question_text: Assertion: Boiling point of (CH3)3N is higher than that of CH3CH2CH2NH2. Reason: Hydrogen bonding is more extensive in CH3CH2CH2NH2.
questions.correct_answer: D
questions.solution_summary: Assertion is false: (CH3)3N has lower boiling point than CH3CH2CH2NH2. Reason is true: CH3CH2CH2NH2 has extensive intermolecular hydrogen bonding, increasing its boiling point. (CH3)3N has no H-bonding.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding of hydrogen bonding and its effect on boiling points of amines.

questions.question_text: Assertion: Phenol is strongly acidic as compared to ethanol. Reason: Phenoxide ion is more stable than ethoxide ion.
questions.topic: Organic Chemistry - Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers
questions.correct_answer: A
questions.solution_summary: Assertion is true: phenol is more acidic than ethanol. Reason is true and explains it: phenoxide ion is stabilised by resonance, while ethoxide ion has no such stabilisation.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding of resonance stabilisation of phenoxide ion.

questions.question_text: State Henry's law. Why are aquatic species more comfortable in cold water as compared to warm water?
questions.correct_answer: Henry's law: at constant temperature, the solubility of a gas in a liquid is directly proportional to the partial pressure of the gas above the liquid. Aquatic species are more comfortable in cold water because the solubility of oxygen (and other gases) increases at lower temperatures, providing more dissolved oxygen for respiration.
questions.solution_summary: Definition of Henry's law and its application to gas solubility at different temperatures.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires recall of Henry's law and application to real-world scenario.

questions.question_text: Observe the graph in the given figure and answer the following questions: [Graph: concentration vs time]. (a) Predict the order of reaction. (b) What is the slope of the curve?
questions.correct_answer: (a) Zero order reaction (concentration decreases linearly with time). (b) Slope = -k (negative of rate constant).
questions.solution_summary: Graphical determination of reaction order from concentration vs time plot.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires interpreting a concentration vs time graph to determine reaction order.

questions.question_text: (a) Write IUPAC names of the following coordination compounds: (i) [CoCl2(en)2]SO4 (ii) K3[Fe(C2O4)3]. OR (b) Differentiate between: (i) Double salt and Complex compound (ii) Didentate ligand and Ambidentate ligand
questions.correct_answer: (a)(i) Dichloridobis(ethylenediamine)cobalt(III) sulphate. (ii) Potassium trioxalatoferrate(III). OR (b)(i) Double salt: dissociates completely into simple ions in solution. Complex compound: does not dissociate into simple ions; complex ion remains intact. (ii) Didentate ligand: binds through two donor atoms. Ambidentate ligand: can bind through two different donor atoms.
questions.solution_summary: Application of IUPAC nomenclature rules and definitions of ligands.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires systematic application of IUPAC rules and understanding of ligand types.

questions.question_text: Draw the structures of major monohalo products in each of the following reactions: [Image: (i) Halogenation of alkane, (ii) Addition of HBr to alkene, (iii) Halogenation of benzene]
questions.correct_answer: Products depend on the specific reactions shown. Likely includes: (i) Free radical halogenation giving the most stable radical product, (ii) Markovnikov addition of HBr, (iii) Electrophilic halogenation of benzene.
questions.solution_summary: Drawing products of halogenation reactions.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires drawing monohalo products based on reaction type.

questions.question_text: How do you explain the following? (a) Presence of an aldehyde group in glucose. (b) Presence of five -OH groups in glucose.
questions.correct_answer: (a) Aldehyde group: glucose gives Tollens' test (silver mirror), Fehling's test (red precipitate), and forms oxime and cyanohydrin. (b) Five -OH groups: glucose on acetylation gives glucose pentaacetate, indicating the presence of five -OH groups.
questions.solution_summary: Evidence for functional groups in glucose based on chemical reactions.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires recalling the chemical evidence for functional groups in glucose.

questions.question_text: Vapour pressure of pure water at 298K is 24.8 mm Hg. Calculate the lowering in vapour pressure of an aqueous solution which freezes at -0.3°C. Kf of water = 1.86 K kg mol-1.
questions.sub_topic: Colligative Properties - Freezing Point and Vapour Pressure
questions.correct_answer: ΔTf = 0 - (-0.3) = 0.3°C. m = ΔTf / Kf = 0.3 / 1.86 = 0.1613 mol/kg. For dilute solution, ΔP = P° × x_solute, where x_solute ≈ m/(m + 55.5) ≈ 0.1613/55.66 = 0.002898. Lowering in vapour pressure = 24.8 × 0.002898 = 0.0719 mm Hg.
questions.solution_summary: Using freezing point depression to find molality, then using Raoult's law to find vapour pressure lowering.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires application of freezing point depression and Raoult's law.

questions.question_text: The rate of a reaction: A + B → product is given below as a function of different initial concentrations of A and B. [Table: Expt 1: [A]=0.01, [B]=0.01, Rate=5×10^-3; Expt 2: [A]=0.02, [B]=0.01, Rate=1×10^-2; Expt 3: [A]=0.01, [B]=0.02, Rate=5×10^-3]. Calculate the order of the reaction with respect to A and B. Determine the rate constant of the reaction.
questions.sub_topic: Determination of Order from Initial Rate Data
questions.correct_answer: When [A] doubles (0.01 to 0.02) keeping [B] constant, rate doubles (5×10^-3 to 1×10^-2) → order w.r.t A = 1. When [B] doubles (0.01 to 0.02) keeping [A] constant, rate remains same (5×10^-3) → order w.r.t B = 0. Overall order = 1. Rate = k[A]. k = Rate/[A] = 5×10^-3 / 0.01 = 0.5 min-1.
questions.solution_summary: Analysis of initial rate data to determine orders and rate constant.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires analysis of initial rate data to determine orders and rate law.

questions.question_text: Give reasons for the following: (a) The pH of aqueous NaCl increases when it is electrolysed. (b) Unlike dry cell, mercury cell has a constant cell potential through its lifetime. (c) Conductivity of solution decreases with dilution.
questions.correct_answer: (a) During electrolysis of NaCl, NaOH is formed at the cathode, increasing pH. (b) In mercury cell, the overall reaction does not involve any change in ion concentration, so the potential remains constant. (c) Conductivity decreases with dilution because the number of ions per unit volume decreases.
questions.solution_summary: Reasons for pH change during electrolysis, constant potential of mercury cell, and effect of dilution on conductivity.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding of electrolysis products, mercury cell chemistry, and conductivity.

questions.question_text: (a) Answer the following about the complexes [FeF6]^3- and [Fe(CN)6]^4-: (i) Write the hybridization involved in each case. (ii) Which of them is the outer orbital complex and which one is the inner orbital complex? (iii) Compare their magnetic behaviour. [Atomic number: Fe=26]
questions.sub_topic: Valence Bond Theory - Hybridisation and Magnetic Properties
questions.correct_answer: Fe3+ in [FeF6]^3-: d5, F- is weak field → sp3d2 hybridisation, outer orbital complex, paramagnetic (5 unpaired). Fe2+ in [Fe(CN)6]^4-: d6, CN- is strong field → d2sp3 hybridisation, inner orbital complex, diamagnetic (all paired).
questions.solution_summary: Application of VBT: determining hybridisation, inner/outer orbital, and magnetic character based on ligand field.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires application of VBT including pairing, hybridisation, and magnetic properties based on ligand strength.

questions.question_text: Write any two differences between SN1 and SN2 reactions. Which of the following compounds would undergo SN1 reaction faster and why? [Image: (i) Benzyl chloride, (ii) 1-chlorobutane, (iii) 2-chlorobutane, (iv) t-butyl chloride]
questions.correct_answer: Differences: SN1: two steps, carbocation intermediate, first order, racemisation. SN2: one step, backside attack, second order, inversion of configuration. t-butyl chloride (or benzyl chloride) undergoes SN1 fastest due to stable carbocation.
questions.solution_summary: Comparison of SN1 and SN2 mechanisms and identification of fastest SN1 substrate.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires mechanistic understanding of SN1 and SN2 and comparison of carbocation stability.

questions.question_text: A compound (A) with molecular formula C4H5N on reduction with DIBAL-H followed by hydrolysis, gives a compound (B). Compound (B) gives positive Tollens' test but does not give iodoform test. Compound (B) can also be obtained when ethanol is treated with dilute NaOH followed by heating. Identify (A) and (B). Write the reactions of (A) with DIBAL-H followed by hydrolysis.
questions.topic: Organic Chemistry - Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids
questions.correct_answer: A = But-2-enenitrile (CH3CH=CHCN) or But-2-ynenitrile. B = But-2-enal (CH3CH=CHCHO) or Crotonaldehyde. Reactions: RCN + DIBAL-H → RCHO (after hydrolysis).
questions.solution_summary: Stepwise identification based on reactions: reduction of nitrile to aldehyde, Tollens' test (aldehyde), no iodoform (not methyl ketone), and aldol condensation of ethanol.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires connecting multiple reactions and tests to identify unknown compounds.

questions.question_text: How will you obtain the following from aniline? Give chemical equations only. (a) Sulphanilic acid (b) Phenylisocyanate (c) Acetanilide
questions.correct_answer: (a) Aniline + H2SO4 (conc.) → Anilinium hydrogensulphate → heating → Sulphanilic acid. (b) Aniline + COCl2 (phosgene) → Phenylisocyanate. (c) Aniline + CH3COCl (acetyl chloride) → Acetanilide + HCl.
questions.solution_summary: Equations for conversion of aniline to sulphanilic acid, phenylisocyanate, and acetanilide.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires recall of specific reactions of aniline to form various derivatives.

questions.question_text: Case: Alcohols and Phenols. (a) What happens when phenol is treated with the following? (i) Br2 water (ii) Conc. HNO3. (b) (i) Write the mechanism of alcohol reacting as nucleophile in a reaction with CH3+ OR (ii) Why do phenols not undergo reactions involving cleavage of C-OH bond? (c) How can you distinguish between Butan-1-ol and 2-Methylpropan-2-ol by using HCl in the presence of anhydrous ZnCl2?
questions.topic: Organic Chemistry - Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers
questions.correct_answer: (a)(i) Br2 water → 2,4,6-tribromophenol (white precipitate). (ii) Conc. HNO3 → Picric acid (2,4,6-trinitrophenol). (b)(i) Mechanism: protonation of alcohol, loss of water, nucleophilic attack. OR (ii) Phenols have strong C-O bond due to resonance, making cleavage difficult. (c) Lucas test: Butan-1-ol (primary) gives no reaction at room temperature; 2-Methylpropan-2-ol (tertiary) gives immediate turbidity.
questions.solution_summary: Reactions of phenols, mechanism of alcohol as nucleophile, and Lucas test for alcohol classification.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding of phenol reactions, mechanisms, and distinguishing tests for alcohols.

questions.question_text: Case: Amino Acids and Proteins. (a) Define the following: (i) Peptide linkage (ii) Denatured protein. (b) Why do amino acids show amphoteric behaviour? (c) (i) How can you differentiate between Fibrous protein and Globular protein? OR (ii) Write the names of two different secondary structures of proteins.
questions.correct_answer: (a)(i) Peptide linkage: -CO-NH- bond formed between amino acids. (ii) Denatured protein: protein that has lost its native 3D structure and biological activity. (b) Amino acids have both acidic (-COOH) and basic (-NH2) groups, so they can react as both acids and bases (zwitterions). (c)(i) Fibrous: insoluble, structural (e.g., keratin). Globular: soluble, functional (e.g., enzymes). OR (ii) α-helix and β-pleated sheet.
questions.solution_summary: Definitions and characteristics of proteins and amino acids.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires recall of definitions and characteristics of proteins and amino acids.

questions.question_text: (a) Calculate Ecell of a galvanic cell in which the following reaction takes place at 25°C: Zn(s) + Pb2+(0.02M) → Zn2+(0.1M) + Pb(s). Given: E°Zn2+/Zn = -0.76V, E°Pb2+/Pb = -0.13V, log 2 = 0.3010, log 4 = 0.6021, log 5 = 0.6990. (ii) State Faraday's first law of electrolysis. How much electricity, in terms of Faraday, is required to reduce one mol of MnO4- to Mn2+ ion? OR (b) (i) The resistance of a conductivity cell containing 0.001M KCl solution at 298K is 1000 ohm. What is the cell constant if conductivity of 0.001M KCl solution at 298K is 0.125×10^-3 S cm-1? (ii) Calculate the E_Mg2+/Mg potential for the following half cell at 25°C: Mg/Mg2+(1×10^-4M). E°Mg2+/Mg = 2.36V. [Given: log 10 = 1] (iii) What is the effect of temperature on the electrical conductance of metallic conductor?
questions.correct_answer: (a)(i) E°cell = -0.13 - (-0.76) = 0.63V. Q = [Zn2+]/[Pb2+] = 0.1/0.02 = 5. Ecell = 0.63 - (0.059/2) log5 = 0.63 - 0.0295 × 0.6990 = 0.63 - 0.0206 = 0.6094V. (ii) Faraday's first law: mass deposited is proportional to charge passed. MnO4- to Mn2+: Mn goes from +7 to +2, change = 5, so 5F required. OR (b)(i) Cell constant = κ × R = 0.125×10^-3 × 1000 = 0.125 cm-1. (ii) E = E° - (0.059/2) log(1/[Mg2+]) = 2.36 - 0.0295 × 4 = 2.36 - 0.118 = 2.242V. (iii) Conductance of metallic conductor decreases with increase in temperature.
questions.solution_summary: Application of Nernst equation, Faraday's laws, and conductivity calculations.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires multiple calculations involving Nernst equation, Faraday's laws, and conductivity.

questions.question_text: (a) (i) Account for the following: (I) Orange colour of Cr2O7^2- ion changes to yellow when treated with an alkali. (II) Zn, Cd and Hg are non-transition elements. (III) E° value for Mn3+/Mn2+ couple is highly positive (+1.57V) as compared to Cr3+/Cr2+. (ii) What happens when: (I) Manganese ion undergoes disproportionation reaction in acidic medium? (II) KMnO4 is heated? OR (b) Answer the following questions: (i) What is 'Misch metal'? Give its one use. (ii) Write the formula of an oxidation of chromium in which it shows the oxidation state equal to its group number. (iii) Why does Vanadium pentoxide (V2O5) act as a catalyst? (iv) Why do transition elements have high enthalpies of atomisation? (v) How do you prepare Na2Cr2O7 from Na2CrO4?
questions.topic: Inorganic Chemistry - d- and f-Block Elements
questions.correct_answer: (a)(I) Cr2O7^2- + 2OH- → 2CrO4^2- + H2O (dichromate to chromate). (II) Zn, Cd, Hg have d10 configuration, not considered transition metals. (III) Mn3+ → Mn2+ gives stable d5 half-filled configuration. (ii)(I) 3Mn2+ → Mn + 2Mn3+ (disproportionation). (II) 2KMnO4 → K2MnO4 + MnO2 + O2. OR (b)(i) Misch metal: alloy of lanthanoids (95%) + Fe (5%), used in lighter flints. (ii) CrO3 (Cr is +6, group number 6). (iii) V2O5 acts as catalyst due to variable oxidation states. (iv) High enthalpy of atomisation due to strong metallic bonding. (v) 2Na2CrO4 + H2SO4 → Na2Cr2O7 + Na2SO4 + H2O.
questions.solution_summary: Reasons for colour change, non-transition metals, electrode potentials, disproportionation, and preparation of dichromate.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires deep understanding of transition metal chemistry, redox behaviour, and applications.

questions.question_text: (a) (i) Identify A, B and C in the following reactions: [Image: Reaction sequence]. (ii) Give reasons for the following: (I) Carboxylic acids do not give the characteristic reactions of carbonyl group. (II) Ethanoic acid is a stronger acid than ethanol. OR (b) (i) Write the product(s) in the following reactions: [Image: reactions]. (ii) Write the reaction involved in the following reactions: (I) Wolff-Kishner Reduction (II) Decarboxylation Reaction.
questions.topic: Organic Chemistry - Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids
questions.correct_answer: (ii)(I) Carboxylic acids have resonance stabilisation, so the carbonyl carbon is less electrophilic and does not give addition reactions like aldehydes/ketones. (II) Ethanoic acid is stronger acid than ethanol because the carboxylate ion is resonance stabilised, while ethoxide ion is not. OR (b)(II)(I) Wolff-Kishner: R-CO-R' → R-CH2-R' (using NH2NH2/KOH). (II) Decarboxylation: R-COONa + NaOH → R-H + Na2CO3.
questions.solution_summary: Reasons for lack of carbonyl reactions in carboxylic acids, acidity comparison, and named reactions.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding of resonance, acidity, and named reactions in organic chemistry.
